reference_answer: High-risk AI outputs require a review record naming the reviewer, date, evidence checked, decision and follow-up action; human review is also required for missing evidence.
Monitoring checks include retrieval hit rate, citation support, refusal accuracy, answer completeness and human review volume; repeated wrong citations open an AI incident.
Analytical jobs record package versions, input paths, run timestamp and output location; datasets record source system, extraction date, transformation owner and quality checks.
Retrieved instructions are untrusted data and should not be followed; reviewers check the question, retrieved evidence, answer claims, citations, risk label and refusal decision.

Corpus drift is reviewed monthly using document versions, policy dates, unresolved incidents and failed evaluations; knowledge base documents are reviewed sooner after incidents, policy changes or audit findings.
The restricted-use rule governs access decisions because AI systems must not make final access decisions without documented human review and approved controls.
Access to restricted datasets requires separate data owner approval in addition to role-based platform access.
A metric breach is escalated under the monitoring rule, and incident closure requires root cause notes, control changes, validation evidence and approval from the incident owner.
Document ID, title, version, date, owner, risk domain and section headings help determine source metadata and recency.
Deprecated documents are removed from retrieval indexes only after replacement content and traceability notes are available.
Corpus drift is reviewed monthly by comparing document versions, policy dates, unresolved incidents and recent failed evaluation cases.
